title: Chapter 5: The Old Chapel Pact
content: The Old Chapel stood at the far edge of campus like a rotting tooth in an otherwise perfect smile. Heath had found it three weeks into his first term, following the sound of nothing — the silence that gathered in places God had stopped visiting.

He led Cat through the treeline at midnight, their breath fogging in front of them like ghostly signals. She didn't ask where they were going. That was the thing about Cat: she followed when the direction felt right, and she stopped when it didn't. No hesitation. No second-guessing. He'd never met anyone who trusted her own instincts so completely, and he'd never met anyone whose instincts were so worth trusting.

The door groaned when he pushed it. Dust lifted in the candlelight — because someone had been here before them, someone who always left a single votive burning on the altar. Heath had never found out who. The flame was the only light in the chapel, a single orange tongue licking at forty years of stale air, and it painted the rafters in shadows that moved like living things.

Cat stepped inside. Her eyes swept the space — the overturned hymnals, the warped floorboards, the pews scarred with decades of carved graffiti. Initials. Dates. Crude symbols. A serpent eating its own tail. A cross turned upside down. The letters *M.R.W. + D.L.K., 1987* carved deep into oak by some long-dead student who'd probably been just as angry, just as trapped. A whole lineage of desperate children had left their marks in this wood.
She ran her finger along the carvings without flinching.
"Sit with me," Heath said.

They took the front pew. The wood was cold even through their coats, cold the way things got when they hadn't been warmed by human contact in years. The candle threw their shadows against the altar — two tall, thin shapes kneeling before a God who wasn't home. The smell hit him the way it always did: old wood and older secrets, candle wax gone rancid, the faint sweetness of decay that lived in the bones of abandoned buildings.
Heath didn't waste time.

"I overheard the faculty laughing." His voice was low, flat — the voice he used when his anger was so compressed it had become something like granite. "About us. The scholarship students." He looked at the candle flame, watched it bend and shiver. "They called us 'diversity inventory.'"
He let the words hang in the stale air. They were worse than any slur. Inventory. Stock. Product on a shelf, warehoused until the optics required display.

Cat didn't react. Not the way he expected — no gasp, no flare of nostrils. Just a slow blink, the kind that happened when something you already knew was confirmed with a violence you hadn't braced for.
"I know," she said.
He turned. "You *knew*?"

"I knew they thought it. I didn't know they said it out loud. That's — bolder than I expected." She folded her hands in her lap, and the gesture was so composed, so Vance, that it almost masked the tremor in her fingers. Almost. "They're getting comfortable."
"Comfortable," Heath repeated. The word tasted like ash.

"My father's doing. He makes them feel safe. Protected. Like the rules are suggestions and the law is a negotiation." Cat stared at the altar — not at the cross above it, but at the bare wood of the table, the scratched surface where communion wine had probably stained the grain decades ago. "The money is rotting from the inside, Heath. The new library — it was a shell game. Capital campaign raised twelve million, construction cost four, and the rest disappeared into Vance family management fees. 'Consulting.' 'Advisory services.'" She said the words like she was reading from an indictment. "My father uses this school as a personal bank. And the old families let him because they're all doing it. Every last one of them."
Heath listened. The candle crackled — a tiny sound, a single syllable of fire.

"The academy is a machine," Cat continued. Her voice had gone distant, clinical, like a surgeon describing a tumor she'd studied but hadn't yet cut. "It runs on broken children. Takes them in damaged, polishes the damage into something marketable, and spits out alumni who fund the next generation of wreckage. They don't want us whole. They want us profitable." She paused. "And I'm supposed to be its next product."
She turned to look at him, and in the candleglow her face was all angles and shadows, the softness burned away, leaving something hard and bright and furious underneath.
"My father doesn't see people. He sees roles. Props." Her voice cracked on the last word — just barely, a hairline fracture she immediately sealed. "I'm his finest prop."
Heath stood.

He couldn't sit anymore. The pew felt like a cage, the chapel like a waiting room for the life they were supposed to accept — quiet, grateful, small. He paced the aisle, his boots grinding dust into the floorboards, and the sound was like years being ground down to nothing.
He stopped in front of the altar. Turned.

Cat was watching him from the pew, the candlelight caught in her dark eyes, and she looked like something from an older century — a woman in a portrait, painted by someone who understood that beauty and rage were the same color.
"So let's stop being props."

The words came out before he'd fully formed them, but they were right. He felt them land in his chest like a dropped key — the exact fit for a lock he'd been carrying since the day Robert's charity check arrived with its perfect handwriting and its perfect distance and its perfect *nothing*.
"You be my claws, I'll be your reason." He held her gaze. "I'll tear down what you point at, and you'll tell me where the load-bearing walls are."

Cat rose. Not slowly — she was on her feet in one fluid motion, as if she'd been waiting for a signal she hadn't known she needed. She crossed to him, and he saw her hands were shaking now, not from cold, not from fear, but from the effort of holding back something enormous.
She reached for his hand.
Her fingers closed around his knuckles — his split, bloody, scarred knuckles against her smooth, manicured ones. The contrast was obscene. It was also the most real thing he'd ever felt.
"I'll give you purpose," she said. "You'll give me permission."
He didn't breathe. The candle flame bent between them like a witness.
The blood seal was his idea.

He found it on the altar — a rusted iron candle holder, one of the old kind with a weighted base and a spiral stem, and near the base, a jagged edge where the iron had cracked and curled. He picked it up. The rust was rough under his fingers, tactile and ancient, and the sharp edge caught the candlelight like a tooth.
"What are you doing?" Cat asked.
He didn't answer with words. He pressed the edge to his left palm, just below the mound of his thumb, and dragged.

Pain — bright, clean, immediate. A line of fire across his flesh. He hissed through his teeth, and the sound echoed off the chapel walls like something sacred. Blood welled up, dark and slow, pooling in the cup of his palm. The smell hit him: copper, hot and thick, the metallic tang of his own body betraying itself, and underneath it, something earthier — iron and age and rust from the candle holder mixing into a cocktail that was pure old world.
He held the makeshift blade out to her.
Cat looked at the edge. At the blood already drying on the iron. At his open hand, dripping onto the altar wood.
She took it without hesitation.

He watched her press the point to her palm — the same spot, the same angle — and draw it across her skin. She flinched. Her jaw tightened. But she didn't make a sound. Blood bloomed in her palm, bright against her pale skin, and she let it drip once, twice, onto the altar beside his.
Then she reached for him.

They pressed their wounds together — his palm to hers, her palm to his. The blood mingled. Wet heat against wet heat, pain against pain, and Heath felt her pulse through the wound, or maybe it was his own pulse, or maybe it was both of them beating at the same frequency for the first time in their lives.

The candlelight caught the red seeping between their fingers — a promise written in a language older than words. Older than the chapel. Older than the school. Older than debt and inheritance and every system designed to turn people into inventory.

Cat raised her other hand and pressed it over their joined palms, sealing them. She looked at him, and her eyes were wet, but she was not crying. This was not sadness. This was the opposite of sadness — this was the terrible, electric clarity of choosing something with your whole body.
"Whatever we do from here," she whispered, "we do together. We don't stop until the walls come down."
"Together," he repeated.
The word tasted like copper. Like rust. Like the beginning of something that would end in fire.
They didn't let go.

Still holding hands, bleeding into each other, they sat on the altar steps like children at the foot of a throne they were planning to overthrow. Cat reached behind her and pulled a hymnal from the pile — *Hymns of Grace and Glory*, the cover cracked, the pages yellowed. The paper crinkled as she opened it, a dry papery whisper that sounded almost alive, and she turned to the back cover where blank space waited.
She pulled a pen from her coat — of course she had a pen, Cat Vance was always prepared to document, even at midnight, even bleeding — and began to draw.

"The Vance family structure." She sketched fast, confident lines. A pyramid. Her father at the top. Below him, her mother — a dotted line, no real authority. Then the board members, the donors, the trustees. Each name connected by lines of influence, dotted lines of debt, solid lines of loyalty. She wrote dollar amounts next to names. She wrote dates. She wrote things she had no business knowing.
Heath watched the diagram bloom like a map of enemy territory. It was beautiful. Not pretty — *beautiful* in the way a weapon is beautiful. Engineered for maximum damage.

"The Academy board," she continued, adding a second structure. "Seven members. Four are Vance-controlled. Two are independent but compromised — financial entanglements. One is hostile to my father but too afraid to move alone."
"Who covers for who?" Heath asked.

She wrote. He leaned in, still pressing his bleeding palm against hers, and added what he knew: names of the boys who bullied with impunity because their fathers endowed the gymnasium. The faculty who looked the other way because their tenure reviews went through the Dean, who answered to the board, who answered to Vance. The security guards who reported incidents that never made it to files. The counselor who "lost" complaints.
"Mrs. Alderman," Heath said. "She's the one who called me 'diversity inventory.' But she's not the source — she's a parrot. Repeats whatever the senior faculty say."
"Then we don't waste time on her," Cat said. She drew a circle around a name near the top of the pyramid: *Dean Whitmore*. "We start with him. He's the keystone. If he falls, the arch collapses."

Heath stared at the diagram. It was architecture. It was a schematic of controlled demolition, and Cat had drawn it from memory — every load-bearing beam, every joint, every weak point where the whole structure would fold if you applied pressure in exactly the right place.

He thought of Robert's check. The perfect handwriting. The distance disguised as generosity. Purpose, he realized, wasn't given. It was taken. It was made — from grief and fury and the refusal to remain furniture in someone else's house.
"Who else?" he asked.

They built it together. Page after page of the hymnal filled with Cat's precise handwriting and Heath's scrawled additions. Names, relationships, amounts. The donor hierarchy — who gave, who laundered, who received. The bullying chain of command — who started it, who amplified it, who gave it institutional cover. They were designing the blueprint of a demolition.

At one point Cat lifted her bleeding palm to her mouth and licked the edge of the wound — instinct, probably, the way you touch a burn to your lips — and she paused. Her tongue touched copper, and something shifted in her expression. Not disgust. Recognition. She tasted it — the blood, the iron, the price — and she didn't flinch.
She put her hand back in his and kept writing.
They left at three in the morning.

The candle had burned down to a stub, the votive glass hot enough to warm the air within arm's reach. The hymnal lay open on the altar, covered in blue ink and rust-brown fingerprints — a sacred text of their own making.

Heath pushed the door open. The snow had come while they were inside, a thin white crust over the world, and the cold hit them like a slap. Their breath came out in clouds. The chapel steps were slick. The campus beyond was sleeping, the spires of the main hall dark against a sky that was just barely beginning to consider the possibility of dawn.
Cat stepped out first. Heath followed. They walked three paces toward the main campus before he stopped.
He turned.
She turned.
"If one of us breaks this," he said, "if one of us chooses them over us — the other walks. No explanations. No forgiveness. Just goes."
The words were cold. They were supposed to be cold. A wire fence around something that couldn't survive being trampled.
Cat looked at him. The snow was settling on her dark hair, on the shoulders of her coat, and she looked like a statue — something carved to commemorate a war that hadn't happened yet.
"Agreed." A beat. "But I won't break it."
"Neither will I."
She held his gaze for a long moment. Then she turned and walked toward the east wing, toward the Vance dynasty, toward the gilded cage with its heated floors and freezing heart.
Heath walked toward the scholarship dorm. The south wing. The place where inventory was stored.

They didn't look back. But their palms were still bleeding — pressed into coat pockets, the wounds stinging against the rough wool, the blood drying into the fabric in shapes that would brown and stiffen and remain. They carried each other's blood now. Under their fingernails. In the creases of their skin. In the fibers of their coats.
Two figures in the snow, walking back to their separate cages with a war plan hidden in their chest.
The campus slept.
The pact did not.
